title: Chef Square Tile Problem

content.problemStatement:
The chef buys square tiles of size 1∗11*11∗1  from N shops. The ith shop sells a[i]a[i]a[i] tiles. The chef wants to know if he can create a large, tiled square area using all the tiles he bought.


Input Format

The first line contains a single integer NNN.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers a[i]a[i]a[i].


Output Format

Output "Yes" if the Chef can create a square area using all the tiles.
Output "No" otherwise.
constraints: The sum of a[i] in a test case fits in a signed 64‑bit integer
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case: first line contains an integer N, the number of shops. Second line contains N space‑separated integers a[i], the number of tiles bought from the i‑th shop.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print "Yes" if the total number of tiles can form a perfect square area, otherwise print "No". Each answer on its own line.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Sum = 4+9+7 = 20, which is not a perfect square.

solution.approach: The only thing that matters is the total number of tiles. Compute the sum S of all a[i] for a test case. The tiles can form a square iff S is a perfect square, i.e., there exists an integer k such that k*k = S. We obtain k by taking the integer square root of S (using Math.sqrt) and verify k*k == S. This runs in O(N) per test case and uses O(1) extra space.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, O(total N) overall.
